news_summary: Recent efforts highlight advancements in human rights through increased access to free prison phone calls and the preservation of Indigenous cultural heritage in Nepal.
news_summary_fr: Des initiatives récentes mettent en lumière les progrès réalisés en matière de droits de l'homme, notamment grâce à un accès élargi aux appels téléphoniques gratuits depuis les prisons et à la préservation du patrimoine culturel autochtone au Népal.
news_summary_es: Las iniciativas recientes ponen de relieve los avances en materia de derechos humanos, gracias a un mayor acceso a las llamadas telefónicas gratuitas desde las cárceles y a la preservación del patrimonio cultural indígena en Nepal.

articles.title: The case for making prison phone calls free: new data, real results
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM For decades, a phone call home from prison could cost a family several dollars a minute. That wasn’t an oversight. It was the business model. A growing number of state prison systems and county jails have moved to make calls free, arguing that regular contact with family is one […]
The post The case for making prison phone calls free: new data, real results first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

For decades, a phone call home from prison could cost a family several dollars a minute. That wasn’t an oversight. It was the business model.

A growing number of state prison systems and county jails have moved to make calls free, arguing that regular contact with family is one of the more reliable tools for reducing harm during and after incarceration. A new report from Worth Rises, a nonprofit that monitors the prison industry, now has numbers on what actually happened.

The numbers are larger than expected

The report covered six state prison systems, California, New York, and the federal system among them, plus more than a dozen county jails across Los Angeles, New York City, and Massachusetts. When agencies negotiated contracts directly with telecom providers rather than routing deals through revenue-sharing arrangements, costs dropped. By a lot: about 62 percent for state systems, 68 percent for jails.

Across those systems, incarcerated people and their families have saved more than $622 million. Most of those savings went to Black and brown families, who are disproportionately represented in the incarcerated population.

Call volume went up sharply. Daily use per person in prisons rose from roughly 25 minutes to nearly 45 minutes once calls became free. In jails, it more than doubled, from about 27 minutes to nearly 57 minutes a day. In total: an estimated 600 million additional calls and 6.4 billion more minutes of connection.

What people talked about changed, too

When calls cost money, families ration them: emergencies, financial logistics, urgent updates. When cost disappears, so does the rationing. People call to check on children. They talk through housing plans before release, work out job details, or pass along the ordinary family news that doesn’t feel urgent enough to justify three dollars a minute.

Researchers have tracked where reentry breaks down, and family ties keep coming up. People who stay connected during incarceration are more likely to find stable housing and work after release, and less likely to return. Worth Rises didn’t produce that finding; it’s fairly well established in the literature. The report just shows how much contact was being suppressed by cost.

Correctional staff at the facilities studied reported something similar from inside. Tensions dropped. Safety improved for staff and incarcerated people.

A practical argument for a values question

Advocates for free prison calls have long argued from a human rights position: incarcerated people should be able to stay connected to their families. That argument is sound. It also hasn’t moved policy as fast as anyone hoped.

The cost argument lands differently. Free calls, properly contracted, actually run cheaper than what came before, because direct contracts cut out the revenue-sharing arrangements with private providers. For legislators weighing budgets, that removes the need to resolve a values debate before acting.

About 330,000 incarcerated people, or roughly 15 percent of the two million people in American jails and prisons, now have access to free calls, video calls, or messaging.

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant policy change making prison phone calls free, which has broad positive impacts on incarcerated individuals and their families, especially marginalized communities. It provides detailed data on cost savings, increased communication, and improved safety, highlighting a meaningful social justice advancement with measurable benefits.
articles.personality_title: Making prison phone calls free saves families money and strengthens connections
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For many years, families had to pay several dollars per minute to talk on the phone with someone in prison. This high cost was part of how phone companies made money from prison calls.

**What happened** – Recently, several states and county jails began offering free phone calls to people in prison. A new report studied six state prison systems and more than a dozen jails, showing that when contracts were made directly with phone companies, call prices dropped by over 60%. This change saved families more than $622 million and led to a big increase in the time prisoners spent talking to their loved ones.

**Impact** – Free calls helped families talk about everyday things, not just emergencies, which made relationships stronger. More phone time also helped prisoners prepare for life after release, like finding jobs and housing. Staff at prisons noticed fewer tensions and safer environments. The biggest savings and benefits went to Black and brown families, who are affected most by incarceration.

**What's next step** – About 15% of people in U.S. prisons and jails now have free access to calls or video chats. The report suggests that more places could adopt this system, using direct contracts to lower costs and improve family connections without extra budget worries.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Offering free prison phone calls lowers costs, increases family contact, and improves safety inside prisons, showing a practical way to support people during and after incarceration.
articles.personality_title_fr: Appels téléphoniques gratuits en prison : économies et liens familiaux renforcés
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Pendant des années, les familles devaient payer plusieurs dollars par minute pour parler au téléphone avec une personne en prison. Ce coût élevé faisait partie du modèle économique des entreprises de téléphonie.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Récemment, plusieurs États et prisons de comté ont commencé à offrir des appels téléphoniques gratuits aux personnes incarcérées. Un nouveau rapport a étudié six systèmes pénitentiaires d'État et plus d'une douzaine de prisons, montrant que lorsque les contrats étaient conclus directement avec les entreprises de téléphonie, les prix des appels ont chuté de plus de 60 %. Ce changement a permis aux familles d'économiser plus de 622 millions de dollars et a fortement augmenté le temps passé au téléphone.

**Impact** – Les appels gratuits ont permis aux familles de parler de choses ordinaires, pas seulement des urgences, ce qui a renforcé les liens. Plus de temps au téléphone a aussi aidé les prisonniers à préparer leur vie après la libération, comme trouver un emploi ou un logement. Le personnel des prisons a remarqué moins de tensions et un environnement plus sûr. Les plus grandes économies et bénéfices ont profité aux familles noires et latinos, les plus touchées par l'incarcération.

**Prochaine étape** – Environ 15 % des personnes en prison ou en centre de détention aux États-Unis ont maintenant accès à des appels ou des vidéos gratuits. Le rapport suggère que d'autres établissements pourraient adopter ce système, en utilisant des contrats directs pour réduire les coûts et améliorer les contacts familiaux sans souci budgétaire.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Offrir des appels gratuits en prison réduit les coûts, augmente les contacts familiaux et améliore la sécurité, montrant une solution concrète pour soutenir les personnes pendant et après leur incarcération.
articles.personality_title_es: Llamadas telefónicas gratuitas en prisión: ahorros y conexiones familiares más fuertes
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante muchos años, las familias pagaban varios dólares por minuto para hablar por teléfono con alguien en prisión. Este alto costo era parte del modelo de negocio de las compañías telefónicas.

**Qué pasó** – Recientemente, varios estados y cárceles del condado comenzaron a ofrecer llamadas telefónicas gratuitas a las personas encarceladas. Un nuevo informe estudió seis sistemas estatales de prisiones y más de una docena de cárceles, mostrando que cuando los contratos se hacían directamente con las compañías telefónicas, los precios bajaron más del 60 %. Este cambio ahorró más de 622 millones de dólares a las familias y aumentó mucho el tiempo que los presos pasaban hablando con sus seres queridos.

**Impacto** – Las llamadas gratuitas permitieron a las familias hablar de cosas cotidianas, no solo emergencias, fortaleciendo así las relaciones. Más tiempo de llamada también ayudó a los presos a prepararse para la vida después de salir, como conseguir empleo y vivienda. El personal de las prisiones notó menos tensiones y un ambiente más seguro. Los mayores ahorros y beneficios fueron para familias negras y latinas, que son las más afectadas por la encarcelación.

**Próximo paso** – Alrededor del 15 % de las personas en prisiones y cárceles de EE. UU. ahora tienen acceso gratuito a llamadas o videollamadas. El informe sugiere que más lugares podrían adoptar este sistema, usando contratos directos para bajar costos y mejorar las conexiones familiares sin preocupaciones presupuestarias.

**Resumen en una frase** – Ofrecer llamadas gratuitas en prisión reduce costos, aumenta el contacto familiar y mejora la seguridad, mostrando una forma práctica de apoyar a las personas durante y después de la encarcelación.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of two vintage rotary phones connected by a glowing thread of light, floating gently between a stylized prison building and a cozy home silhouette, set against a soft, natural color palette that evokes connection, hope, and the breaking down of barriers.

articles.title: Songs of resistance: Nepal’s Indigenous communities fight to preserve their musical heritage
articles.summary: "Pilru — Songs of resistance is a community-led initiative dedicated to documenting, protecting, and reclaiming the handcrafted musical instrument passed across generations by Adivasi Tharu and Kumhar communities of the Tarai"
articles.content:
Pilru, a terracotta musical instrument shaped like birds, made by Indigenous Tharu and Kumhar communities from Nepal’s southern plains, traveled from a village in Western Nepal to an art store in Kathmandu Valley, thanks to an art initiative led by Tharu artist Lavkant Chaudhary.

For generations, Indigenous peoples have been using musical instruments made from clay in ceremonies, social events, and celebrations, not only to amuse and entertain the gathering but also to communicate and connect with deities and souls.

Pilru – Songs of Resistance

“Pilru — Songs of Resistance is a community-led initiative dedicated to documenting, protecting, and reclaiming the Pilru, a handcrafted musical instrument passed across generations by Adivasi Tharu and Kumhar communities of the Tarai,” says artist Lavkant Chaudhary in his statement.

“The project records oral histories, songs, and techniques, centering community voices and asserting the Pilru as a living practice, rather than a passing trend.”

“I learned to play and make Pilru during my childhood,” said Roinu Chaudhary, a 70-year-old man from Deukhar of Lamahi in Western Nepal, in an interview with Global Voices. He was praised for his contribution to preserving the practice of Pilru at the event in Kathmandu. “We used to play it while grazing cattle and going to the jungle.”

“I haven’t seen anyone other than Tharus using this instrument,” said Chhabilal Kopila, a Tharu litterateur and activist, during the event. “It’s a matter of concern that the young generation isn’t interested in this instrument.”

One of the proficient users of Pilru is Santu Chaudhary from Dang in Western Nepal, according to Kopila. He can create music with this instrument for any type of song.

Here’s a clip of Santu Chaudhary playing Pilru:

Xutuli, a similar musical instrument made of either clay or bamboo, is used during the Bihu festival in Assam, India. Made in the shape of a bird, people believe the sound of Xutuli invites rain according to Assamese folk culture.

Misappropriation of traditional knowledge

Lavkant Chaudhary says, “The project also challenges cultural appropriation by non-Indigenous institutions that replicate, market, and strip this art of its meaning without acknowledging its makers.”

Many institutions have been reproducing and selling Indigenous art and cultural products without informing and acknowledging the communities, let alone the Pilru.

“Traditional cultural expressions (TCEs) and traditional knowledge (TK) involve important aspects of the culture and traditions of Indigenous Peoples, who should have the right to prohibit, or allow, the use of this knowledge,” writes Esther Aburto Olague, attorney, OZM Legal & Business Consulting Services, Mexico. “The use by third parties outside the community may even result in an offense to the cultural heritage of these Indigenous Communities.”

Seconding Lavkant’s statement, Kopila wrote on Facebook, “As Tharu and Kumhar communities continue their struggle for recognition, it is crucial to center authorship, dignity, and cultural justice.”

The Indigenous practitioners who are conserving the TCEs and TK will get due credit and returns only when the local law recognizes that they are holders of rights over works, of any kind, where they manifest elements of their culture and identity, as in the case of Mexico.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a community-led initiative by Indigenous Tharu and Kumhar peoples in Nepal to preserve and reclaim their traditional musical instrument, the Pilru. This effort protects cultural heritage, asserts Indigenous rights, and challenges cultural appropriation, which has broad significance for Indigenous communities and cultural justice worldwide. The story provides detailed context about the instrument, its cultural role, and the challenges faced, making it a substantive and inspiring example of cultural preservation and human rights advocacy.
articles.personality_title: Nepal’s Indigenous communities revive Pilru, their traditional musical instrument
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In Nepal’s southern plains, the Indigenous Tharu and Kumhar communities have long used a special clay musical instrument called the Pilru. Shaped like birds, these instruments are part of ceremonies and celebrations, connecting people with their culture and ancestors.

**What happened** – A community-led project called Pilru — Songs of Resistance is working to record, protect, and bring back the use of the Pilru. Led by Tharu artist Lavkant Chaudhary, the initiative collected stories, songs, and making techniques from elders like Roinu Chaudhary, who has played Pilru since childhood. The project also brought the instrument from remote villages to art stores in Kathmandu, raising awareness.

**Impact** – This effort is important because it helps keep a rare cultural practice alive, especially as younger generations lose interest. It also fights against cultural appropriation, where others copy and sell Indigenous art without permission or credit. By documenting the Pilru’s history and music, the project supports the rights of Indigenous people to protect their culture and share it on their own terms.

**What's next step** – The community hopes to inspire more young people to learn and play the Pilru. They also want local laws to recognize Indigenous rights over their traditional knowledge and art. This could help prevent misuse and ensure that communities benefit from their cultural heritage.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Nepal’s Indigenous Tharu and Kumhar communities are reviving the Pilru instrument to protect their culture and assert their rights against cultural appropriation.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les communautés autochtones du Népal relancent le Pilru, leur instrument de musique traditionnel
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Dans les plaines du sud du Népal, les communautés autochtones Tharu et Kumhar utilisent depuis longtemps un instrument de musique en argile appelé Pilru. En forme d’oiseaux, ces instruments font partie des cérémonies et des célébrations, reliant les gens à leur culture et à leurs ancêtres.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Un projet communautaire nommé Pilru — Chants de résistance travaille à enregistrer, protéger et remettre en usage le Pilru. Menée par l’artiste Tharu Lavkant Chaudhary, l’initiative a recueilli des histoires, des chansons et des techniques auprès des anciens comme Roinu Chaudhary, qui joue du Pilru depuis son enfance. Le projet a aussi permis de faire connaître l’instrument dans des boutiques d’art à Katmandou.

**Impact** – Cet effort est important car il aide à préserver une pratique culturelle rare, surtout alors que les jeunes générations perdent l’intérêt. Il lutte aussi contre l’appropriation culturelle, où d’autres copient et vendent l’art autochtone sans autorisation ni crédit. En documentant l’histoire et la musique du Pilru, le projet soutient les droits des peuples autochtones à protéger leur culture et à la partager selon leurs propres règles.

**Prochaine étape** – La communauté espère encourager davantage de jeunes à apprendre et jouer du Pilru. Elle souhaite aussi que les lois locales reconnaissent les droits autochtones sur leurs savoirs traditionnels et leur art. Cela pourrait empêcher les abus et garantir que les communautés bénéficient de leur patrimoine culturel.

**En une phrase** – Les communautés autochtones Tharu et Kumhar du Népal relancent l’instrument Pilru pour protéger leur culture et affirmer leurs droits face à l’appropriation culturelle.
articles.personality_title_es: Las comunidades indígenas de Nepal reviven el Pilru, su instrumento musical tradicional
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En las llanuras del sur de Nepal, las comunidades indígenas Tharu y Kumhar han utilizado durante mucho tiempo un instrumento musical de arcilla llamado Pilru. Con forma de pájaro, estos instrumentos forman parte de ceremonias y celebraciones, conectando a las personas con su cultura y antepasados.

**Qué pasó** – Un proyecto comunitario llamado Pilru — Canciones de resistencia está trabajando para registrar, proteger y recuperar el uso del Pilru. Liderado por el artista Tharu Lavkant Chaudhary, la iniciativa recogió historias, canciones y técnicas de fabricación de ancianos como Roinu Chaudhary, quien ha tocado el Pilru desde niño. El proyecto también llevó el instrumento desde aldeas remotas a tiendas de arte en Katmandú, aumentando la conciencia.

**Impacto** – Este esfuerzo es importante porque ayuda a mantener viva una práctica cultural rara, especialmente cuando las nuevas generaciones pierden interés. También combate la apropiación cultural, donde otros copian y venden arte indígena sin permiso ni reconocimiento. Al documentar la historia y la música del Pilru, el proyecto apoya los derechos indígenas para proteger su cultura y compartirla en sus propios términos.

**Próximo paso** – La comunidad espera inspirar a más jóvenes a aprender y tocar el Pilru. También quieren que las leyes locales reconozcan los derechos indígenas sobre su conocimiento tradicional y arte. Esto podría ayudar a prevenir el uso indebido y asegurar que las comunidades se beneficien de su patrimonio cultural.

**Resumen en una frase** – Las comunidades indígenas Tharu y Kumhar de Nepal están reviviendo el instrumento Pilru para proteger su cultura y afirmar sus derechos frente a la apropiación cultural.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of terracotta bird-shaped Pilru instruments arranged in a circle, glowing softly with earthy tones, surrounded by abstract silhouettes of musical notes and swirling patterns that evoke oral traditions and ancestral voices, set against a simple natural background symbolizing Nepal’s southern plains and the cultural resilience of Indigenous Tharu and Kumhar communities.
